1: Jonah's prayer from inside the fish is the only sustained speech of the book before Jonah 4. It is composed almost entirely of phrases from the Psalter — scholars have identified allusions to Psalms 3, 5, 18, 22, 30, 31, 42, 46, 69, 86, 88, 116, 120, 130, and 142. This dense intertextuality with lament psalms suggests that the prayer was composed as a deliberate anthology of psalm language, placed on Jonah's lips to make his individual crisis resonate with the whole tradition of Israelite lament and praise.
2: Min beten sheol shivati — 'from the belly of Sheol I cried.' Sheol is the Hebrew underworld, the realm of the dead. Jonah describes the fish's belly as equivalent to Sheol — he was, from his perspective, as good as dead. The parallel 'belly of the fish' / 'belly of Sheol' is deliberate: the fish is simultaneously rescue and entombment, salvation and death-house. This ambivalence is central to the sign Jesus appropriates: the tomb is both death and preparation for resurrection.
3: The imagery of waves and breakers passing over him (kol mishbarecha vegalecha alay avaru) closely echoes Psalms 42:7 ('all your breakers and your waves have gone over me'). The address to YHWH ('you cast me into the deep') frames the drowning not as accident or the sailors' act but as YHWH's doing — Jonah perceives divine agency even in the consequence of his own rebellion. This is theologically mature: he does not blame the sailors.
4: 'I am driven away from your sight' (nigrazti mineged einecha) — the very condition Jonah sought by fleeing. The bitterest irony: his flight from YHWH's presence has now resulted in an experience of being cut off from it that he did not actually want. The exile from God's presence that Jonah chose becomes something he laments. 'Yet I shall again look upon your holy temple' — this anticipatory confidence in restoration, placed amid the depths, is the theological hinge of the psalm.
5: The 'waters encompassed me to the point of death' (afu mayim ad nefesh) — literally 'waters were up to my soul/life.' The deep (tehom) surrounds him — the same primordial deep (tehom) over which the Spirit hovered in Genesis 1:2, now threatening to swallow one of God's own prophets. Seaweed wrapped around his head — the realism of drowning in the sea before the fish swallows him is vividly described.
6: The descent to 'the roots of the mountains' and 'the bars of the earth forever' (beriche eretz le'olam) — the bars/bolts of Sheol are a common ancient Near Eastern image for the gates of the underworld (cf. Job 38:17; Psalms 107:16). Jonah has reached the lowest structural point of the cosmos. The pivot: vata'al mishachat chayai YHWH Elohai — 'yet you brought up my life from the pit, O YHWH my God.' The reversal — descent followed by ascent — is the structural template for the resurrection.
7: 'When my life was fainting away, I remembered YHWH, and my prayer came to you, into your holy temple.' The phrase behit'ateif alay nafshi ('when my life was ebbing away') appears also in Psalms 142:3 and Lamentations 2:12. The act of remembering (zachar) YHWH triggers prayer which reaches the temple — the earthly location of YHWH's presence that Jonah had tried to flee by going to sea. The temple serves as the conduit for prayer from any location, including the depths of the sea.
8: 'Those who pay regard to vain idols forsake their steadfast love (chesed).' The contrast with the Gentile sailors' newly offered vows (Jonah 1:16) is sharp: those who worship idols are described as forsaking chesed — the covenant love of YHWH — while the very sailors Jonah might have dismissed as pagans have just offered vows to YHWH. Jonah's theology here is conventional orthodox Israelite polemic; the narrative context subverts it by showing Gentiles worshiping YHWH while the prophet languishes in a fish.
9: 'But I with the voice of thanksgiving will sacrifice to you; what I have vowed I will pay. Salvation belongs to YHWH!' (lYHWH hayeshuah.) This climactic declaration — 'the salvation is YHWH's' — is structurally placed at the psalm's conclusion as its theological thesis. It also announces the theme of the whole book: who controls salvation? Who may receive it? The Gentile Ninevites, the sailors, and eventually Jonah himself are all objects of YHWH's salvific mercy. The answer to 'whose salvation?' is YHWH alone.
10: YHWH commands the fish and it vomits Jonah onto dry land. The verb vayaqo ('vomited') is undignified — perhaps intentionally so. Jonah has been marinating in the fish's belly for three days; the book is not trying to beautify his ordeal. The 'dry land' (hayabashah) echoes the creation language of Genesis 1:9-10 and the sea narrative in Jonah 1 ('who made the sea and the dry land,' Jonah 1:9) — Jonah is restored to the ordered world of creation after his journey through the watery chaos.
